fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out this spell-checking tool for source code that we talked about in the last planning session. The big complaint from devs is that existing spell checkers are totally useless on code — they flag every identifier, URL, hex code, whatever, as a misspelling and people just turn them off. We need something smarter.

Basically it should be able to break apart compound names (like the camelCase stuff, underscored names, etc.) into real words before checking, and it should know to skip over technical garbage like URLs and email addresses and file paths. It also needs to actually understand code structure for different languages — so it only checks the parts humans wrote as words, not keywords and operators and all that.

There should be a way to get spelling suggestions for bad words, and the end-to-end check should support some kind of override list so teams can whitelist their own domain terms. Also teams sometimes have naming conventions that look like misspellings — there should be a way to define patterns to skip those too.

For the architecture, we want this structured properly — not one giant file. Think about how we did the config layering on the auth module last quarter, similar vibe for extensibility. The I/O adapter should be totally separate from the core logic. Output should be byte-accurate positions so editors can use it directly.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: fn spellcheck(text: &str, lang: Option<&str>, allow: Option<&[&str]>, ignore_patterns: Option<&[&str]>) -> Vec<Misspelling>

trigger_keywords: how should camelCase be split
how do you split identifiers
what are the splitting rules for compound names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Splits happen at: (1) a lowercase letter immediately followed by an uppercase letter (case hump); (2) inside a run of uppercase letters, where the last capital of the run attaches to the following lowercase word — so an acronym like 'XML' directly before 'Http' splits as XML|Http; (3) at underscores, periods, colons, and hyphens — these separators are consumed and never emitted as words; (4) at every transition between letters and digits. Any segment consisting only of digits is discarded entirely. Each surviving word is reported with its UTF-8 byte start offset, end offset, and text.

trigger_keywords: what technical strings should be masked
which patterns are skipped automatically
what gets ignored in skip mode
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The built-in skip patterns cover: web URLs (http/https schemes), email addresses, hex color codes introduced by a leading '#', absolute filesystem paths, and long hexadecimal hashes. Any word that is fully or partially covered by one of these masked spans is dropped from results; ordinary words around them are still emitted.

trigger_keywords: how does case-insensitive dictionary lookup work
does capitalization matter for dictionary entries
how is dictionary membership checked
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A query word is considered known when its lowercased form equals the lowercased form of any dictionary entry. The original casing on either side is irrelevant. The output for each query is: the word exactly as given, a space, then 'true' or 'false', in the same order the queries were supplied.

trigger_keywords: how should suggestion casing work
what casing do correction candidates use
how are suggestions formatted for uppercase or lowercase queries
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Candidate casing is adapted to match the query word's casing: an all-uppercase query uppercases every candidate, an all-lowercase query lowercases every candidate, and a mixed- or title-cased query leaves the candidate text unchanged. At most five candidates are returned, one per line, in ranked suggestion order.

trigger_keywords: which parts of code are checked
what regions are extracted from source
what does the source parser extract
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The syntax-aware parser extracts only: declared names (function names, parameter names, local bindings, type names, field names), comment text, and the contents of string literals. Language keywords, operators, punctuation, and numeric literals are excluded. If the language identifier is unrecognized, the engine falls back to treating the input as plain prose rather than failing.

trigger_keywords: what does the allowlist do
how do I whitelist domain terms
how does the allow field work in spellcheck
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'allow' field in a spellcheck request accepts a list of accepted words. Any query word whose lowercased form is present on the allowlist is treated as correctly spelled and never reported, even if it is absent from all dictionaries. Words not on the allowlist and not in any dictionary are still flagged. The request shape is {"op":"spellcheck","text":<text>,"lang":<id>,"allow":[words...]}.

trigger_keywords: how do ignore patterns work
can I skip words matching a regex
how do I suppress naming convention false positives
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'ignore_patterns' field in a spellcheck request accepts a list of regular expressions. Any word whose text matches one of those patterns is skipped and never reported as a misspelling, in addition to the built-in technical-string skip patterns. Words that match no pattern and are unknown to the dictionaries are still reported. The request shape is {"op":"spellcheck","text":<text>,"lang":<id>,"ignore_patterns":[regexes...]}.

trigger_keywords: how should byte offsets be computed
what kind of offsets are reported
are offsets character positions or byte positions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All reported positions are UTF-8 byte offsets into the original input string — not character indices or codepoint indices. Both start and end offsets must lie on valid UTF-8 boundaries. This must remain correct in the presence of multi-byte and multi-codepoint characters. Each output line is: <start_byte> <end_byte> <word>, ordered by position.

trigger_keywords: how should the code be structured
what modules should exist
how do we separate concerns in the architecture
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The codebase must be organized into a multi-file directory tree (e.g. src/ with cohesive modules, tests/) — not a single monolithic file. Distinct logical units are required for: identifier word splitting, skip-pattern masking, source-region extraction, dictionary membership, suggestion generation, output formatting, and end-to-end orchestration. The JSON I/O adapter must be completely decoupled from the core domain logic. The core engine must depend on dictionary and configuration abstractions, not concrete loaders, so new languages or skip patterns can be added without modifying the core.

trigger_keywords: what operations are supported
what are the valid op values
what commands does the system accept
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The system supports five operation types via the 'op' field: 'split' (identifier splitting, optionally with a 'lang' for source extraction), 'skip' (technical-string masking and word extraction), 'check' (word-list dictionary membership), 'suggest' (correction suggestions), and 'spellcheck' (end-to-end pipeline). Each operation has a defined request shape documented in the feature specifications.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values

what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'split', 'skip', 'check', 'suggest', 'spellcheck'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: how we did the config layering on the auth module last quarter, similar vibe for extensibility
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Open/Closed Principle requirement: adding a new source language or a new skip pattern must not require modifying the core checking engine. This is achieved by depending on a dictionary abstraction and a configuration abstraction (DIP), and by registering language parsers and skip patterns as pluggable extensions rather than hardcoded branches in the engine.

context_pointers.vague_reference: The I/O adapter should be totally separate from the core logic
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The JSON stdin/stdout adapter (the execution adapter) is solely responsible for parsing JSON commands and rendering text output. The core spell-checking domain logic must have no dependency on stdin, stdout, or JSON parsing — this is the Strict Separation of Concerns / Anti-Overfitting NFR described in the architecture section of start.md.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific guidance on that detail right now — please check the existing codebase structure and the feature test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the canonical expected behavior, and align your implementation to match those contracts exactly.

feature1_identifier_splitting.json.description: A single contiguous identifier or token is broken into its constituent words. Splits occur at lowercase-to-uppercase humps, inside uppercase runs (an uppercase run keeps its trailing capital with the following lowercase word, so an acronym immediately before a capitalized word is separated correctly), at underscores, periods, colons and hyphens (these separators are consumed and never emitted), and at every transition between letters and digits. Segments made only of digits are discarded. Each surviving word is reported as its UTF-8 start byte offset, end byte offset, and text, one per line, ordered by position. The request is {"op":"split","text":<token>} with no language.

feature2_skip_patterns.json.description: Before tokenizing prose, spans matching built-in skip patterns for technical strings are masked out, so any word fully or partially covered by such a span is removed from the results while ordinary surrounding words are still emitted. The default patterns cover web URLs, email addresses, hex color codes introduced by a leading hash sign, absolute filesystem paths, and long hexadecimal hashes. Each surviving word is reported as its UTF-8 start byte offset, end byte offset, and text, one per line, ordered by position. The request is {"op":"skip","text":<text>}.

feature3_dictionary_lookup.json.description: A plain word-list dictionary is built from a list of entries and answers membership queries case-insensitively: a query word is known when its lowercased form equals the lowercased form of any entry, regardless of the original casing on either side. For each query word the result is the word, a space, and either true (known) or false (unknown), one per line, in the order the query words were given. The request is {"op":"check","dict":[entries...],"words":[queries...]}.

cases.expected_output:
hello true
WORLD true
rust true
python false

feature4_suggestions.json.description: Given a misspelled word, an ordered list of at most five correction candidates is produced from the bundled English affix/dictionary fixture. The casing of the candidates follows the casing of the query word: an all-uppercase query upper-cases the candidates, an all-lowercase query lower-cases them, and mixed or title casing leaves candidate text unchanged. Each candidate is printed on its own line in suggestion order. The request is {"op":"suggest","word":<word>}.

feature5_code_extraction.json.description: When a source language is supplied, a syntax-aware parse first extracts only the spell-checkable regions of code: declared names (function, parameter, local binding, type, field names), comment text, and the contents of string literals. Language keywords, operators, punctuation and numeric literals are not extracted. The extracted regions are then run through the same word splitter (case humps, separators, letter/digit transitions, digit-only segments discarded). Each resulting word is reported as its UTF-8 start byte offset within the whole input, end byte offset, and text, one per line, ordered by position. The request is {"op":"split","text":<source>,"lang":<id>}.

feature6_1_prose_spellcheck.json.description: Plain prose is checked end to end: the text is tokenized into words and every word that is not found in the active dictionaries is reported as a misspelling, while correctly spelled words produce no output. Each misspelled word is reported as its UTF-8 start byte offset, end byte offset, and text, one per line, ordered by position; correctly spelled input yields empty output. The request is {"op":"spellcheck","text":<text>,"lang":"text"}.

input.text: the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog

feature6_2_source_spellcheck.json.description: Source code is checked end to end: the spell-checkable regions (declared names, comments, string literals) are extracted with a syntax-aware parse, split into words, and each word not found in the active dictionaries is reported as a misspelling. Keywords, syntax, numbers, and dictionary-known words produce no output, so dotted call targets and standard identifiers are not flagged. The supplied language id selects the parser. Each misspelling is reported as its UTF-8 start byte offset within the whole input, end byte offset, and text, one per line, ordered by position. The request is {"op":"spellcheck","text":<source>,"lang":<id>}.

input.text:
def calculat_user_age(bithDate) -> int:
            # This is an examle_function that calculates age
            usrAge = get_curent_date() - bithDate
            userAge

feature6_3_skip_in_context.json.description: During an end-to-end check, technical strings embedded in prose or source are masked before tokenizing, so the letters inside web URLs, email addresses, hex color codes, and absolute filesystem paths are never reported as misspellings even though they are not real words. Ordinary misspelled words around them are still flagged. Each reported misspelling is its UTF-8 start byte offset, end byte offset, and text, one per line, ordered by position. The request is {"op":"spellcheck","text":<text>,"lang":"text"}.

input.text:
Visit https://www.exampl.com/badspeling for more info.
        But this actualbadword should be flagged.

Contact usr@exampl.com or admin@badspeling.org
        This misspelledword should be flagged though.

feature6_4_allowlist.json.description: An end-to-end check can be given an allowlist of accepted words; any query word whose lowercased form is on the allowlist is treated as correctly spelled and never reported, even if it is not in any dictionary. Words not on the allowlist and not in any dictionary are still reported. Each reported misspelling is its UTF-8 start byte offset, end byte offset, and text, one per line, ordered by position. The request is {"op":"spellcheck","text":<text>,"lang":"text","allow":[words...]}.

input.text:
ok words
        testword
        good words
        actualbad

feature6_5_ignore_patterns.json.description: An end-to-end check can be given a list of regular expressions; any word matching one of the supplied patterns is skipped and never reported as a misspelling, in addition to the built-in technical-string patterns. Words that match no pattern and are unknown to the dictionaries are still reported. Each reported misspelling is its UTF-8 start byte offset, end byte offset, and text, one per line, ordered by position. The request is {"op":"spellcheck","text":<text>,"lang":"text","ignore_patterns":[regexes...]}.

input.text:
This text has HTML and CSS frameworks.
        Also customword and testpattern should be ignored.
        But badword and anotherbadword should be flagged.
